title: How a Mom's 'Silly' Trivia Business Became a Community-Building Empire
guestBio: Christina Wooldridge is the founder of Trivia Goat, a live entertainment company bringing tech-enabled trivia to bars, breweries, and restaurants. With a background in hotel/restaurant management and nonprofit fundraising, she's a working mom of two based in Orange County, CA, who turned her unconventional business idea into a thriving community-centered company.
excerpt: Discover how Christina Wooldridge transformed her 'silly' trivia idea into a thriving live events company that builds real community. If you're wondering whether your unconventional business idea is worth pursuing, this conversation will give you the permission you need to start.
metaDescription: Learn how a mom entrepreneur turned her unconventional trivia business into a thriving community-building company. Hospitality leadership tips included.
keyTakeaways: Hospitality is not a soft skill — it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation.
Your business idea doesn't have to be 'prestigious' to be valuable and profitable.
The real thing that keeps customers coming back is human connection, not just products or services.
Scaling doesn't always mean more — sometimes it means packaging your expertise differently.
Motherhood sharpens your leadership through intentionality and focus, forcing you to find long-term solutions.
Your worth isn't tied to your productivity — you can create value through presence and community building.
Unconventional ideas often outperform serious ones because they solve real problems people actually have.

content:
What if the business idea you're dismissing as 'too silly' or 'not serious enough' is actually the one that could change everything? Christina Wooldridge, founder of Trivia Goat, proves that unconventional ideas often outperform the prestigious ones we think we should pursue.

In this powerful conversation on The Manage Her Podcast, Christina shares how she transformed what seemed like a simple trivia business into a community-building empire that's revolutionizing how venues connect with their customers. Her story isn't just about business success — it's about giving yourself permission to pursue ideas that might seem unconventional but solve real problems people actually have.

From Childhood Parades to Building Community

Christina's entrepreneurial journey has a golden thread that runs from childhood to today: creating experiences that bring people together. As a child, she organized neighborhood parades and events, unknowingly developing the skills that would later become her superpower. This natural inclination toward hospitality and community building became the foundation for everything that followed.

Her path wasn't linear. She moved through hotel and restaurant management, then into nonprofit fundraising, each experience teaching her more about what really matters in business: human connection. When she became a mother, everything shifted. Suddenly, the quick fixes and band-aid solutions that might have worked before weren't enough. Motherhood forced her to think long-term and find sustainable solutions.

The Leadership Superpower You're Probably Undervaluing

One of the most striking insights from Christina's story is how she reframes hospitality. This isn't a 'soft skill' — it's a leadership superpower. In her words, hospitality skills create real power in any conversation because they're fundamentally about making people feel seen, heard, and valued.

Think about the last time you walked into a room and someone made you feel genuinely welcome. That feeling didn't happen by accident. It happened because someone understood the art of hospitality and used it intentionally. Christina has built an entire business around this principle, and it's working because she's solving a problem most venue owners don't even realize they have.

Venue owners often focus on getting customers in the door, but they misunderstand what keeps them coming back. It's not just the food, drinks, or even the entertainment. It's the human connection. It's feeling like you belong somewhere, like you're part of something bigger than just a transaction.

Scaling Without Sacrificing What Matters

As a working mother, Christina faced the challenge every mom entrepreneur knows intimately: how do you scale a business that depends on your personal presence without sacrificing your role as a mother? Her solution was brilliant in its simplicity. Instead of trying to be everywhere at once, she developed a self-serve model that packages her expertise and systems in a way that others can implement.

This approach allowed her to grow Trivia Goat without constantly trading time for money or missing important moments with her children. She discovered that scaling doesn't always mean doing more — sometimes it means packaging what you already do differently.

The key was recognizing that her real value wasn't just in showing up to host trivia nights. Her value was in understanding how to create belonging, how to make strangers feel like friends, and how to turn a regular Tuesday night into something people look forward to all week.

The Moment Everything Clicked

There was a pivotal moment when Christina realized she wasn't just running trivia nights — she was creating belonging. She watched her hosts become actual friends with each other and saw customers develop genuine relationships that extended beyond the weekly games. That's when she understood she had built something much more valuable than she initially imagined.

This realization changed how she thought about her business and her role in it. She wasn't just an entertainer or event coordinator. She was a community architect, designing experiences that brought people together in meaningful ways. Once she saw this, everything else fell into place.

The Permission You Need to Pursue Unconventional Ideas

Perhaps the most important message from Christina's story is this: give yourself permission to explore the unconventional. Your business idea doesn't need to sound impressive at dinner parties or look good on a LinkedIn headline. It needs to solve a real problem for real people.

Christina's 'silly' trivia business is thriving because it addresses something fundamental that many businesses overlook: our deep human need for connection and community. In a world where we're more digitally connected but often more isolated than ever, creating spaces for genuine human interaction isn't silly — it's essential.

If you're sitting on an idea that seems too simple, too fun, or too unconventional, Christina's story is your permission slip to explore it. The market doesn't need another serious, prestigious business that looks impressive but doesn't create real value. It needs more businesses like Trivia Goat that prioritize human connection and community building.

Managing Yourself First

Throughout our conversation, Christina emphasized something that resonates deeply with every woman trying to build something meaningful: you have to manage yourself first. For her, this means prioritizing mental health, maintaining her running routine, and recognizing that her worth isn't tied to her productivity.

This mindset shift was crucial for her success. When she stopped trying to prove her value through constant busy-ness and started focusing on creating genuine impact, everything changed. She became more intentional with her time, more strategic with her decisions, and more present for both her business and her family.

Christina Wooldridge's story is proof that the business ideas we dismiss as too simple or too silly are often the ones with the greatest potential. By following her instincts, leveraging her natural gifts, and staying focused on what really matters — human connection — she's built something that goes far beyond profit. She's created belonging, one trivia night at a time.
